Heckscher-Ohlin Model 19/34

Theorems
The H-O Theorem: A country will have comparative
advantage in, and therefore will export, that good whose
production is relatively intensive in the factor with which that
country is relatively well endowed.
The Rybczynski Theorem: At constant world prices, if a
country experiences an increase in the supply of one factor, it
will produce more of the product whose production is
intensive in that factor and less of the other.

chunks.section: The Factor Price Equalization Theorem: Given all the
chunks.text:
The Factor Price Equalization Theorem: Given all the
assumptions of the H-O model, free international trade will
lead to the international equalization of individual factor
prices.
!3 reasons why this might fail...
1If countries have di
erent technologies of production.
2Needs complete convergence of the prices of goods. In the real
world, prices of goods are not fully equalized by international
trade { both natural barriers (such as transportation costs)
and government (such as tari
s, import quotas, and other
restrictions).
3We also need the countries to produce the same set of goods.
One possible reason this isn't the case is countries have
radically di
erent ratios of capital to labor or of skilled to
unskilled labor.

Heckscher-Ohlin Model 22/34

Testing the Heckscher-Ohlin Model
The
rst test of the HeckscherOhlin theorem was performed by
economist Wassily Leontief in 1953.
Leontief supposed correctly that in 1947 the United States
was abundant in capital relative to the rest of the world.
Leontief assumed that U.S. and foreign technologies were the
same due to the limited data on foreign technology, which is
consistent with HO theorem.
Thus, from the HeckscherOhlin theorem, Leontief expected
that the United States would export capital-intensive goods
and import labor-intensive goods.

chunks.section: What Leontief actually found, however, was just the opposite
chunks.text:
What Leontief actually found, however, was just the opposite:
the capital-labor ratio for U.S. imports was higher than the
capitallabor ratio found for U.S. exports.
This
nding contradicted the HeckscherOhlin theorem and
came to be called Leontief's paradox .
Heckscher-Ohlin Model 23/34

Leontief's (1953) Test

Exports Imports
Capital ($ million) $2.5 $3.1
Labor (person-years) 182 170
Capital/labor ($/person) $13,700 $18,200
U.S. and foreign technologies are not the same.
By focusing only on labor and capital, Leontief ignored land.
Labor should have been disaggregated by skill (since it would
not be surprising to
nd that U.S. exports are intensive in
skilled labor).
The data for 1947 may be unusual, since World War II had
just ended.
The U.S. was not engaged in free trade, as the HO model
assumes.
Heckscher-Ohlin Model 24/34

Understanding Leontief's Paradox
As with any result that seems counterintuitive, you should
always ask yourself a couple questions.
Is the methodology correct? In particular, if the methodology
is appealing to a theoretical model, were the crimes
committed in moving from theory to data signi
cant?
If the methodology is correct, are there alternative
explanations outside the model which might explain the
result?
If exploring both questions leads you to accept the
methodology and the result, then you have a real puzzle
As you will notice, there is a common theme in economics
that \puzzles" are really just bad methodology or bad models
(smell test usually sucient to identify a faux puzzle)

The Stolper-Samuelson Theorem: Free international trade
bene
ts the abundant factor and harms the scarce factor.
Heckscher-Ohlin Model 31/34

Opinions Toward Free Trade
In the short-run speci
c-factors model, labor may gain or lose
from free trade, but the speci
c factor in the export sector
gains, and the speci
c factor in the import sector loses.
If labor also earns some part of the rental on the speci
c
factor in their industry, the industry of employment of workers
will a
ect their attitudes toward free trade.
In the long-run Heckscher-Ohlin model, however, the industry
of employment should not matter.
According to the Stolper-Samuelson theorem, an increase in
the relative price of exports will bene
t the factor of
production used intensively in exports and harm the other,
regardless of the industry in which these factors of production
actually work.
Heckscher-Ohlin Model 32/34

Opinions Toward Free Trade
In the United States, export industries tend to use high-skilled
labor intensively for research and development and other
scienti
c work.
Therefore, an increase in the relative price of exports will
bene
t skilled labor in the long run, regardless of whether
these workers are employed in export-oriented industries or
import-competing industries.
In the long run, then, the skill level of workers should
determine their attitudes toward free trade.
Heckscher-Ohlin Model 33/34

Theorems
The H-O Theorem: A country will have comparative
advantage in, and therefore will export, that good whose
production is relatively intensive in the factor with which that
country is relatively well endowed.
The Rybczynski Theorem: At constant world prices, if a
country experiences an increase in the supply of one factor, it
will produce more of the product whose production is
intensive in that factor and less of the other.

chunks.text:
The Factor Price Equalization Theorem: Given all the
assumptions of the H-O model, free international trade will
lead to the international equalization of individual factor
prices.
!3 reasons why this might fail...
1If countries have di
erent technologies of production.
2Needs complete convergence of the prices of goods. In the real
world, prices of goods are not fully equalized by international
trade { both natural barriers (such as transportation costs)
and government (such as tari

restrictions).
3We also need the countries to produce the same set of goods.
One possible reason this isn't the case is countries have
radically di
erent ratios of capital to labor or of skilled to
unskilled labor.

Heckscher-Ohlin Model 22/34

Testing the Heckscher-Ohlin Model
The
rst test of the HeckscherOhlin theorem was performed by
economist Wassily Leontief in 1953.
Leontief supposed correctly that in 1947 the United States
was abundant in capital relative to the rest of the world.
Leontief assumed that U.S. and foreign technologies were the
same due to the limited data on foreign technology, which is
consistent with HO theorem.
Thus, from the HeckscherOhlin theorem, Leontief expected
that the United States would export capital-intensive goods
and import labor-intensive goods.

chunks.section: What Leontief actually found, however, was just the opposite
chunks.text:
What Leontief actually found, however, was just the opposite:
the capital-labor ratio for U.S. imports was higher than the
capitallabor ratio found for U.S. exports.
This
nding contradicted the HeckscherOhlin theorem and
came to be called Leontief's paradox .

By focusing only on labor and capital, Leontief ignored land.
Labor should have been disaggregated by skill (since it would
not be surprising to
nd that U.S. exports are intensive in

The data for 1947 may be unusual, since World War II had

The U.S. was not engaged in free trade, as the HO model

Understanding Leontief's Paradox
As with any result that seems counterintuitive, you should
always ask yourself a couple questions.
Is the methodology correct? In particular, if the methodology
is appealing to a theoretical model, were the crimes
committed in moving from theory to data signi
cant?
If the methodology is correct, are there alternative
explanations outside the model which might explain the
result?
If exploring both questions leads you to accept the
methodology and the result, then you have a real puzzle
As you will notice, there is a common theme in economics
that \puzzles" are really just bad methodology or bad models
(smell test usually sucient to identify a faux puzzle)

The Stolper-Samuelson Theorem: Free international trade
bene
ts the abundant factor and harms the scarce factor.
Heckscher-Ohlin Model 31/34

Opinions Toward Free Trade
In the short-run speci
c-factors model, labor may gain or lose
from free trade, but the speci
c factor in the export sector
gains, and the speci
c factor in the import sector loses.
If labor also earns some part of the rental on the speci
c
factor in their industry, the industry of employment of workers
will a
ect their attitudes toward free trade.
In the long-run Heckscher-Ohlin model, however, the industry
of employment should not matter.
According to the Stolper-Samuelson theorem, an increase in
the relative price of exports will bene
t the factor of
production used intensively in exports and harm the other,
regardless of the industry in which these factors of production
actually work.
Heckscher-Ohlin Model 32/34

Opinions Toward Free Trade
In the United States, export industries tend to use high-skilled
labor intensively for research and development and other
scienti
c work.
Therefore, an increase in the relative price of exports will
bene
t skilled labor in the long run, regardless of whether
these workers are employed in export-oriented industries or
import-competing industries.
In the long run, then, the skill level of workers should
determine their attitudes toward free trade.
Heckscher-Ohlin Model 33/34

We have now seen 4 standard theoretical trade models

Adam Smith!Ricardian!Speci

chunks.text:
First, there are some important results from the Melitz model:
Even without gains from variety, countries can gain from trade
though better efficiency.
The most efficient (and biggest) are the firms who export
(which has been the source of numerous empirical research)
But perhaps more importantly, this has given us a tractable
model to allow for more realistic extensive margin...
which can have important implications for models from the
past (Krugman vs. Chaney), but also give us the tools to ask
really interesting questions.

Helpman, Melitz, and Yeaple (2004 AER)
Introduced the option to become a multinational in addition
to exporting.
Everything very similar except firms could now pay a fixed
cost fM>fX>fDto participate in FDI.
This results in the most productive firms being MNEs, the
“medium” productive firms exporting, and the least
productive firms selling only to the domestic market.
This is confirmed in the data and they find that the
productivity advantage of MNEs over exporters is about 15%
for US firms.
International Trade and Heterogeneous Firms 35/42

Cole and Davies (2011 EER)
Investigates how the presence or threat of FDI affects a
country’s “optimal” tariff.
Ellingsen and W¨ arneryd (1997 IER) have homogeneous firms
and show how the “optimal” tariff (one that maximizes
domestic firm profits) is one that is just low enough to
prevent firms from tariff-jumping.
Firm heterogeneity dulls this knife-edge result and allows for
some firms to be MNEs while others export.
They assume firms are heterogenous over fixed cost instead of
marginal cost and don’t allow for drawing productivity from a
distribution.
They find that the presence of FDI lowers the noncooperative
Nash tariff.
International Trade and Heterogeneous Firms 36/42

The H-O Theorem: A country will have comparative
advantage in, and therefore will export, that good whose
production is relatively intensive in the factor with which that
country is relatively well endowed.
The Rybczynski Theorem: At constant world prices, if a
country experiences an increase in the supply of one factor, it
will produce more of the product whose production is intensive
in that factor and less of the other.

chunks.section: The Factor Price Equalization Theorem: Given all the
chunks.text:
The Factor Price Equalization Theorem: Given all the
assumptions of the H-O model, free international trade will
lead to the international equalization of individual factor prices.
The Stolper-Samuelson Theorem: Free international trade
benefits the abundant factor and harms the scarce factor.

Distributional Effects of Trade
Autor, Dorn and Hanson (2013 AER) – Effect of China shock
Pierce and Schott (2016 AER) – Effect of China entering the
WTO
McLaren and Hakobyan (2016 REStat) – Effect of NAFTA
Main takeway is non-college educated workers in particular in
manufacturing (but also in non-manufacturing sectors) made
worse off in terms of employment and wages. College educated
workers saw little to no effect.

New Trade Theory
Intra-industry trade
Can have everything identical (but also have increasing returns
to scale; e.g. add a fixed cost to production and constant
marginal cost) and gain from trade through gains in variety.

New New Trade Theory
New Trade Theory, but with firm heterogeneity.
Now we don’t necessarily gain from trade through increased
variety, but we do gain from increased efficiency by “killing
off” the least productive firms.

Classical Model of Trade: Ricardian 1/42

De
nitions
Marginal Product of Labor: The extra output obtained by
using one more unit of labor.
Absolute advantage: The ability of a country to produce a
good using fewer productive inputs than is possible anywhere
else in the world; or producing the most output with the same
amount of inputs.
Comparative advantage: A country has comparative
advantage in good xif it gives up less of good yto produce
one unit of good x.
Classical Model of Trade: Ricardian 2/42

Assumptions
1. Perfect Competition in all markets.
2. Factors of production cannot move between countries.
3. There are no barriers to trade in goods.
4. Exports must pay for imports.
5. Labor is the only relevant factor of production in terms of
productivity analysis or costs of production.
6. Production exhibits constant returns to scale between labor
and output.
7. Constant MPL .
8. Foreign variables will be denoted with a `'.
Classical Model of Trade: Ricardian 3/42

chunks.section: First Theory for Why Countries Trade and Gain from Trade
chunks.text:
First Theory for Why Countries Trade and Gain from Trade
Adam Smith, the \
rst" economist provides an explanation in
his 1776 book, The Wealth of Nations . Countries can gain by
specializing in what they do well and then trading for other
goods that they do not produce as easily.
In fact, we all do this as individuals. I specialize in being a
professor and then trade these services to get food, clothing,
housing, etc. In contrast, imagine a world where we all had to
be self-sucient.
Smith laid out a simple narrative to show how this works. The
following is a simple numerical model that captures the main
elements of his argument.
Suppose that there are just two countries, England and
Portugal, and two goods, Cloth and Wheat. And the following
chart shows how much output 1 labor produces of each good
in each country.
Classical Model of Trade: Ricardian 4/42

Cloth Wheat
England 1 yard 1/4 bushel
Portugal 1/2 yard 1/3 bushel
Autarky Scenario: Assume each country has 24 units of
labor (called their Endowment ). Suppose they put half their
time into each good.
England: Cloth = 12 yards; Wheat = 3 bushels
Portugal: Cloth = 6 yards; Wheat = 4 bushels
World: Cloth = 18 yards; Wheat = 7 bushels
Classical Model of Trade: Ricardian 5/42

Cloth Wheat
England 1 yard 1/4 bushel
Portugal 1/2 yard 1/3 bushel
Specialization and Trade Scenario: First, let each country
specialize in what they do well (i.e., their absolute advantage).

Take away: If countries specialize in the good they have an

chunks.section: The Political Economy of Trade: A Preliminary View
chunks.text:
The Political Economy of Trade: A Preliminary View
We now have a model that highlights the fact that some win
from trade liberalization (export sector) and some lose from it
(import competing sector).
There has always been this tension in society. Since there are
some losers, does this mean we should not trade?? Here are
three reasons why economists tend not to stress the income
distribution e

ects of trade:
1Income distribution e
ects are not speci
c to international
trade.
2It is always better to allow trade and compensate those who
are hurt by it than to prohibit trade.
3The losers are typically better organized than those who gain.
A bit more on this later, but there is the Trade Adjustment
Assistance Program. For a nice discussion on it's e

International Labor Mobility
Just like free labor mobility within a country will equalize real
wages, free labor mobility across countries will equalize real
wages.
This assumes
That labor is identical { though this isn't that big of an
assumption because you could simply say that wages across
job type will equate.
That labor doesn't care where it lives { think of the EU, there
is theoretically free labor mobility, though people tend to stay
in their own country.
Generally, this raises world output/welfare, but of course there
will be some losers if these gains aren't redistributed somehow.
The same is true for all factors of production { though this
can be dicult for other reasons.
Speci
c-Factors Model 17/19

Recap
We now have a model that
1doesn't rely on exogenous di
erences in technology to drive
comparative advantage and thus trade.
2doesn't result in everyone being better o
without
redistribution (the country as a whole is still better o
with
trade).
3doesn't result in full specialization if the TOT is di
erent than
autarky prices.
This opens the door for understanding the political economy
of trade (winners vs. losers)
